title: Ambient Intelligence & Context-Awareness
body:
**Ambient Intelligence (AmI)** is a multidisciplinary area aiming to make everyday environments *intelligent and adaptive*: a network of "hidden interfaces" that understands human behavior and adapts the environment to user needs. It sits at the intersection of **Pervasive Computing** and **Artificial Intelligence**, and embodies Mark Weiser's vision (1988) that "the most profound technologies are those that disappear."

**Built on distributed pervasive systems.** A *distributed system* is a set of connected independent nodes appearing as one coherent system. A *pervasive system* adds two features: (1) *unconventional, possibly mobile nodes* (smartphones, smart appliances, sensors); (2) *adaptiveness* to the current context. These systems are *volatile*: device/communication failures, bandwidth changes, and dynamic creation/destruction of associations between software components.

**The five AmI components:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| Sensing | Capture info about environment/users |
| Acting | Physically affect the environment (lights, thermostat, robots) |
| Reasoning | Model/recognize behavior — connects sensing and acting |
| HCI | Natural interaction (gestures, speech, emotions) |
| Security & Privacy | Protect sensitive sensed data |

**Context-awareness.** Dey (2001): *context is any information used to characterize the situation of an entity* (person, place, object) relevant to the user-application interaction — "all the data useful to adapt a service." A system is *context-aware* if it uses context to provide relevant services; *adaptiveness* is the property of adjusting behavior to context. Quality is tied to the **awareness mismatch** (human vs. perceived context) — the goal is to minimize it.

**Low- vs high-level context.** Low-level = directly from sensors or simple processing/fusion (e.g., averaging, "Hot&Humid"). High-level = derived via AI models (e.g., "cooking a meal", mood). *Context history* enables deriving (trajectories) and predicting context.

**Domotics** is IoT in the home, enabling automations (event + condition → action) plus healthcare monitoring, energy management, anomaly detection.

**AmI AI challenges:** unbalanced data, labeled-data scarcity, personalization (patterns vary per subject/home), continual learning (drift + forgetting), privacy, resource-constrained devices.

**Exam angle:** Know Dey's definition verbatim, the two pervasive-system features, the five components (especially Reasoning bridging sensing/acting), low vs high-level context examples, and why personalization is harder than in computer vision.

title: IoT & Sensor Data Management
body:
The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a set of physical "smart" objects equipped with sensors, software and connectivity, collecting and exchanging data over a network to achieve a goal. AmI is built on IoT.

**Transducers, sensors, actuators.** A *transducer* converts one form of energy into another. *Sensors* are **input** transducers (physical phenomenon → electronic signal); *actuators* are **output** transducers (energy → action: LEDs, speakers, relays, motors).

- *Analog sensors* output a continuous voltage → need an **ADC** (analog-to-digital converter).
- *Digital sensors* output a digital signal (ON/OFF or built-in ADC).
- *Analog actuators* need a **DAC** (digital-to-analog converter); digital actuators are two-state or have a built-in DAC.

**Sensor categories:** *inertial* (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer), *environmental* (barometer, photometer, thermometer), *position* (orientation, magnetometer). **Virtual sensors** are services turning low-level into high-level context (GPS → weather/semantic location/transit).

**Architecture.** Devices → **gateway** (collects from nearby devices, optional local DB/processing, forwards to cloud) → cloud. The *four-stage* architecture inserts **edge nodes**: more powerful than gateways, near the network, used to reduce latency and pre-process data. **Matter** standardizes how devices talk. IoT types: Consumer, Commercial, Industrial (predictive maintenance), Infrastructure (smart cities/grids).

**Energy is the key constraint.** Communication dominates: transmitting **1 bit ≈ 1000 CPU operations**. Hence *duty cycling* (antenna sleeps), sampling, and mobility-based cost distribution.

**Four sensor data management tasks:** Data Acquisition, Data Cleaning, Query Processing, Data Compression.

**Acquisition strategies:**

| Approach | Idea |
|---|---|
| Batch | Buffer all on sensor, offline processing; precise but high comms + delay |
| Pull | User sets interval/frequency |
| Push | Gateway+sensors agree on a model (linear/Markovian); send only deviations |
| In-network | Overlay network aggregates (avg, min/max) at intermediate nodes |

**Compression** approximates streams: PCA (constant per segment, split when `|v1-v2| < ε`), **PMC-MR** (constant per segment while `max(s)-min(s) <= 2ε`), sliding/overlapping windows (features for activity recognition), Fourier/wavelet transforms.

**Cleaning** removes erroneous readings (dead batteries, dirt, failures, vandalism); done *after* acquisition — but always keep raw data.

**Exam angle:** Know ADC vs DAC and input/output transducers, the 1-bit-≈-1000-ops rule, push vs pull vs in-network, and the PMC-MR `2ε` condition.

title: Time Series Analysis
body:
**What is a time series?** A collection of observations made sequentially in time, each tagged with the instant it was obtained. In Ambient Intelligence every sensor continuously monitors a physical process, so its output *is* a time series. A **univariate** series tracks one variable; a **multivariate** series tracks several (a combination of univariate series) — the common AmI case (e.g. accelerometer + temperature + door contact).

**Continuous vs digital.** Real processes are continuous, so they are **sampled** at a rate in Hz. This is a trade-off: high rates cost energy (sampling + transmitting) and computation; low rates risk information loss.

**Time vs frequency domain.** Time-domain analysis is intuitive and good for linear trends and seasonalities, but struggles with complex non-linear patterns and with separating noise. Frequency-domain analysis (via **Fourier** or **Wavelet** transforms) exposes the constituent frequencies, at the cost of added complexity and signal-processing expertise.

**Preprocessing pipeline:**

| Step | Methods |
|---|---|
| Alignment | time synchronization, resampling to a common rate |
| Imputation | backward/forward fill, mean/median, interpolation (order matters!) |
| Outlier detection | point (global/local) vs subsequence outliers — for *cleaning*, distinct from anomaly *detection* |
| Denoising | SMA $SMA_t=(x_t+\dots+x_{t-N})/N$, EMA $EMA_t=\alpha x_t+(1-\alpha)EMA_{t-1}$, median filter, FFT low/high-pass |
| Normalization | scale each dimension separately (e.g. to [0,1]) so big-range dims don't dominate |

Window size matters: too large over-smooths (loses info), too small barely denoises.

**Segmentation** cuts the infinite stream into windows: fixed-size (time-based `w` seconds, or event-based last `k` events, with overlap `o`) or **dynamic** (boundaries at distribution changes — adaptive but harder).

**Feature extraction.** Handcrafted features (time/frequency statistics) feed classic ML (RF, SVM, MLP, K-Means); **automatic** extraction uses DNNs to learn an **embedding**. Feature reduction helps high-dimensional data and visualization.

**Advanced models (deck 04).** **1D CNNs** capture spatial patterns but miss temporal relations; **RNNs** handle sequences but suffer vanishing/exploding gradients (`input × w^len`); **LSTMs** add a weight-free cell state (long-term) plus a hidden state (short-term). **Seq2Seq** (encoder→context vector→decoder) does forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, embeddings, classification; trained via teacher forcing vs free running. **Attention** adds per-step paths; **Transformers** drop LSTMs entirely, using Q/K/V self-attention (dot-product similarity + softmax), masked decoder attention, multi-head attention, and sine/cosine **positional encoding**.

**Why central to AmI:** sensor streams *are* time series, so every downstream task (activity classification, clustering, anomaly detection, forecasting) starts here.

**Exam angle:** Know the definitions cold (univariate/multivariate, point vs subsequence outlier, the SMA/EMA formulas, vanishing vs exploding gradient with a numeric example), and be ready to contrast paired concepts (time vs frequency domain, fixed vs dynamic segmentation, handcrafted vs automatic features, teacher forcing vs free running, CNN vs RNN, attention vs self-attention).

title: Indoor Localization
body:
Indoor Positioning Systems (IPS) estimate the (x, y) coordinates of a person, object or robot on a building map, usually framed as a **regression problem**. They power navigation, Ambient Assisted Living and object finding. **Why not GPS?** Indoors the GPS signal is weak and gets attenuated/scattered by roofs, walls and objects, giving an average error of 5 to 10 m, far too coarse when many applications need sub-meter accuracy.

**Signal-based approaches.** Most IPS use radio technologies. WiFi (802.11) is ubiquitous and needs no ad-hoc infrastructure, but APs are made for communication, so accuracy is limited. BLE beacons broadcast a UUID, are more accurate and energy-efficient than WiFi, but deployment (count, placement, height, battery) is tricky. UWB sends short wideband pulses: least affected by interference/multipath, penetrates obstacles, sub-meter accuracy, but needs costly infrastructure and is not yet in personal devices.

Two paradigms use the **RSSI** (received signal strength, in negative dB; closer to 0 = stronger). (1) **Trilateration**: convert three RSSI values into distances (circles) and intersect them; more than three is multilateration. But RSSI-to-distance is unreliable indoors due to reflections and interference. (2) **Fingerprinting**: in the *offline phase* a person/robot collects <antenna ID, RSSI> fingerprints at known positions, building a radio map of <Position, Fingerprint> pairs; in the *online phase* the user's current RSSI is matched (naive Euclidean distance, or robust kNN / ML regression). The rationale: each location has a characteristic signal vector. **CSI** (per-subcarrier phase/amplitude via OFDM) is richer and more multipath-robust than RSSI. Other methods: AoA, ToA/ToF, TDoA (timing/angle), plus non-radio PIR, computer vision, VLC and inertial **dead reckoning** (drifts linearly, used only for short displacements). Accuracy depends on multipath, obstacles, dynamic environments and precision needs.

**Exam angle:** be ready to contrast trilateration vs fingerprinting (offline/online phases), justify why GPS fails indoors, compare WiFi/BLE/UWB pros and cons, and explain RSSI vs CSI and the timing methods (ToA needs clock sync; TDoA cancels the device-clock bias).

title: Smart Energy
body:
**Smart grids** integrate all users (generators and consumers) and use ICT for real-time, **two-way** information exchange, making the grid self-healing, adaptive and resilient versus the centralized traditional grid. AI tasks include **load forecasting** (short-term: real-time control and demand response; mid-term: dispatch/maintenance; long-term: system planning), **stability assessment** (using Phasor Measurement Units), and **fault detection**.

**Smart metering.** A **smart meter** measures whole-home consumption with one device and bi-directional communication; this is **Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM)**: cheap and easy, but no per-appliance detail. **Smart plugs** sense a single appliance and send data to a home gateway: this is **Intrusive Load Monitoring (ILM)**, very fine-grained but more intrusive and costly. Monitoring loads supports two **conservation strategies**: user-oriented (recognize habits and waste patterns) and appliance-oriented (schedule/shift appliances). Human Activity Recognition and indoor localization derive habits that are matched to energy patterns to drive recommendations or automatic interventions; context like weather, season and time of day matters.

**Load disaggregation (NILM)** solves the inverse problem: observing only the aggregate P(t) = sum of appliance loads + noise, estimate each appliance's load Pi (multi-output regression, evaluated with MAE). Appliances have signatures: On-Off (easiest), Multi-State, Continuously Variable (hardest), and Permanent consumers. The classic pipeline does event detection, feature extraction and load identification; deep learning (denoising autoencoders, transformers) now dominates by learning signatures directly from raw data. One can train single-appliance models (simple but unscalable) or one multi-output model (efficient, shared features, but harder to optimize). Labeling is scarce, mitigated by submetering, simulation or **transfer learning** (domain-adaptation loss).

**Load forecasting in homes** uses an *observation horizon* (past input) to predict a *forecast horizon* (future), via CNN or seq2seq models with contextual features. **Reinforcement learning** schedules appliances: agent = energy-management system, actions = switch off / change power level / defer; reward balances comfort (e.g., Waiting Time Rate) and consumption; exploration risks comfort, so pre-train then fine-tune. **Privacy:** usage data reveals habits, countered by encryption, differential privacy (Laplacian noise) and trusted platforms.

**Exam angle:** contrast NILM vs ILM, define the disaggregation problem and appliance types, justify deep learning over the classic pipeline, distinguish observation vs forecast horizon, and lay out the RL formulation (agent/actions/reward + comfort) and privacy countermeasures.

title: Human Activity Recognition: Foundations
body:
**Human Activity Recognition (HAR)** uses sensing technologies and AI to continuously and unobtrusively infer the activities people perform in daily life — a crucial source of *context information* for Ambient Intelligence systems.

**Activity granularity.** The course distinguishes **physical activities** (low-level body movements such as walking, running, cycling, stairs, usually captured by mobile/wearable devices) from **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** (high-level everyday activities such as cooking, eating, taking medicines, sleeping, usually monitored with environmental smart-home sensors, sometimes combined with wearables).

**The recognition pipeline.** Sensing devices produce **raw data**, which is cleaned/pre-processed and **segmented** into windows. In the *classic* pipeline an explicit **feature-extraction** step feeds a classifier; in the *deep* pipeline pre-processed raw segments go directly to a network that learns features automatically (cleaning and segmentation are still needed). The model's output is a **probability distribution** over the target activities, from which the **most likely activity** is selected — so HAR is fundamentally a **classification** problem over a predefined activity set.

**Sensor modalities.** Mobile/wearable (inertial sensors dominate), environmental (smart-home IoT, including indoor localization), camera-based (very accurate but privacy-intrusive), audio-based (also privacy-intrusive), and wireless sensing (WiFi CSI perturbed by the body; poor cross-environment generalization). These can be fused in **multi-modal HAR**.

**Challenges.** Data distribution heterogeneity — *inter-variability* (across subjects) and *intra-variability* (same subject, different contexts); *sensing heterogeneity* (different devices/placements/home settings); *class imbalance* (mitigated by data augmentation/SMOTE or synthetic data via GANs); the *"OTHER"* class (an infinite space of unforeseen activities); *transitions* between activities; the *personalized vs. generalized* trade-off (hybrid = generalized base fine-tuned on a little target-user data); and *labeled-data scarcity* (scripted vs. in-the-wild acquisition).

**Exam angle.** Be ready to name the pipeline stages and explain how deep differs from classic; define inter- vs. intra-variability; explain why the "OTHER" class and transitions are hard; and contrast scripted vs. in-the-wild data collection with one drawback each.

title: HAR: Mobile & Wearable Sensors
body:
HAR on **mobile/wearable devices** exploits the ubiquitous sensors in smartphones and smartwatches. **Inertial sensors** dominate because they directly reveal physical body movement: the **accelerometer** (acceleration on 3 axes), the **gyroscope** (angular speed on 3 axes, revealing direction changes), and the **magnetometer** (magnetic field on 3 axes). Other contextual sensors — GPS, light, microphone, proximity, physiological (heart rate) — can help. The most common **placements** are a smartphone in the pocket and a smartwatch on the wrist; placement is itself a source of sensing heterogeneity.

**Pre-processing pipeline.** (1) **Data cleaning**: inertial signals are noisy, so smoothing filters (median, low-pass, Gaussian) are applied; optionally the gravity component is removed to obtain **linear acceleration** (using orientation from the gyroscope) that better isolates actual movement. (2) **Segmentation**: usually **sliding windows** with two tunable parameters — segment length and overlap — where overlap is crucial to capture transitions. Simple physical activities need short windows (e.g., 3 s, 50% overlap); complex activities need longer ones; window choice is a delay-vs-accuracy trade-off. Because data is a **multivariate** time series (multiple sensors, multiple axes, possibly multiple devices, each with its own sampling rate), streams must be temporally aligned and clocks synchronized. **Dynamic segmentation** detects change points via unsupervised methods but is computationally costly. Windows are typically labeled with the **prevalent** activity. (3) **Feature extraction**.

**Hand-crafted vs. deep features.** Handcrafted features are **time-based** and **frequency-based** statistics computed per axis per sensor. Deep models extract features automatically: **CNNs** (local dependency + scale-invariance, but weak on temporal relations), **LSTM/BiLSTM** (strong temporal features; BiLSTM reads forward+backward, reducing overfitting), **hybrid CNN-LSTM** (CNN extracts features, LSTM models time — very effective), and encoder-only **Transformers**.

**On-device constraints / deployment.** **Offline** recognition stores data for later processing (favoring accuracy); **online** recognition processes data in real time for responsive context-aware apps. Existing commercial/OS Activity-Recognition APIs offer a limited, often inaccurate, non-personalized activity set — so HAR remains an open research problem.

**Exam angle.** Know each inertial sensor and what it measures; define linear acceleration and why it helps; explain sliding-window parameters and the role of overlap; justify hybrid CNN-LSTM; and contrast offline vs. online recognition.

title: HAR: Smart-Home / Ambient Sensors
body:
Smart-homes instrumented with sensors can continuously monitor residents' high-level activities (ADLs), a key enabler of **ambient assisted living** (e.g. monitoring elderly subjects for early signs of cognitive decline or emergencies).

**Sensing.** The workhorses are *binary* (ON/OFF) environmental sensors that capture the resident's interaction with the home (dense sensing): magnetic sensors on doors/drawers/fridge, PIR motion sensors (which sense *motion only* — a still person is invisible), pressure mats on chairs/beds, sleep mats (phases, snoring, heart rate), and smart plugs (thresholded to ON/OFF). Two intrinsic limits: they are sparse and in fixed positions (coarse localisation) and they cannot identify *which* resident triggered them. Wearables and indoor localization add richer context but require the user to carry a device; cameras/microphones are deliberately excluded as too privacy-intrusive.

**Segmentation.** Binary sensors have *no fixed sampling rate*, so fixed-time windows are inconsistent and often empty. *Event-based windowing* fixes the number of events per window (variable time span), keeping events dense during activity. A common variant classifies the latest event using the previous `k-1` as context, sliding one event at a time.

**Features.** *Handcrafted*: first/last timestamps, temporal span, per-sensor activation count; plus *time-dependency* (a discounted count with decay rate $\chi$ down-weighting distant events) and *sensor-dependency* (a Mutual Information matrix down-weighting consecutive-but-unrelated events at transitions). Past recognised activities can be added as features, weighted by confidence. *Automatic*: deep models need fixed-size inputs, so time-based segmentation returns; events are encoded as binary time series (raw, change-point, last-fired) and fed to LSTM/GRU (GRU is lighter, better on small datasets), LSTM-CNN, or CNNs over an image representation (time on x, one row per sensor). CASAS is a standard dataset family.

**Exam angle.** Be ready to justify event-based vs time-based windowing, explain $\chi$ and the MI matrix, list the binary encodings, and name the modalities excluded for privacy.

title: Multi-Inhabitant HAR
body:
Most HAR work assumes a *single* resident, but real homes hold several people who may cooperate on one activity, act in parallel, or mix group and individual activities. This breaks single-user recognition.

**The data-association problem.** Environmental sensor events (fridge opened, stove on) are *anonymous*: they say what happened, not who did it. With one resident this is trivial; with two or more, each event is ambiguous. Correctly attributing every event to the inhabitant that triggered it is one of the hardest obstacles to real-world deployment. (Example: a chair pressure sensor and the fridge magnetic sensor fire while Alice sits and Bob cooks — which event is whose?)

**Explicit data-driven association.** Train a classifier to attribute each event (e.g. Naive Bayes with $P(R_n)P(E_i\mid R_n)$ where $E_i$ is a spatio-temporal feature vector; or LSTMs), then run single-user HAR per stream. Drawbacks: needs much labeled data and is environment/subject-specific (poor generalisation).

**Implicit association (multi-label).** A single model maps a mixed window to one activity per resident; association happens implicitly. Variants: *Combined Labels* (one label per activity combination — easy but up to $M^N$ labels), *Binary Relevance* (one binary classifier per class — ignores inter-resident dependencies), *Multi-Output Deep Model* (shared layers + one softmax head per resident — captures relationships), and probabilistic models like the *Factorized HMM*. All need data covering all combinations, generalise poorly, and scale badly.

**Context-aware association.** Wearables/positioning give per-user context (identity, location, posture, gesture); rule-based common-sense knowledge checks each event's *context-consistency* and assigns it accordingly — no training needed, but assumes worn devices and is bounded by context accuracy.

**Subject separation.** A weaker form: decide which events come from *different* subjects without naming them. Flavours: supervised seq2seq (needs annotation), unsupervised (mine single-resident statistics + thresholds), knowledge-based (ontology semantic similarity via Least Common Subsumer + temporal similarity normalised by $T_{max}$).

**Exam angle.** Define data association vs subject separation; contrast explicit/implicit/context-aware; know each multi-label method's pro/con and the scalability problems.

title: Incremental / Continual Learning
body:
**Setting.** Static models are trained once on a fixed class set and never evolve, but real sensor streams are non-stationary: they show **concept drift** (a user performs a known activity differently over time, e.g., young vs. elderly), **concept evolution** (new activities/patterns appear), **concept forgetting** (drop patterns no longer representative of the user), and **outliers/anomalies** (rare distant samples — distinguishing a harmless outlier from a true anomaly such as a fall matters). Re-training from scratch is **not scalable**: it needs to store all labeled data and re-run costly training, and it cannot adapt quickly. **Incremental (online) learning** instead updates the model on mini-batches of new data.

**The core problem.** Neural networks suffer **catastrophic forgetting**: training on new data overrides weights that encoded old knowledge, so accuracy on past concepts collapses. This is the **stability–plasticity dilemma** — *stability* = retaining old knowledge, *plasticity* = learning new knowledge — and the goal is a good trade-off (stability without rigidity, plasticity without chaos). **Continual learning** studies learning from an infinite stream while balancing the two.

**Method families.** (1) **Replay/rehearsal**: keep a small buffer of old labeled samples (random, exemplars near class means, or constrained) and mix it with new data; cheap and easy but needs storage and may overfit the buffer. **Generative replay** (e.g., HAR-GAN) synthesizes old-like data instead of storing it, at higher complexity. (2) **Regularization**: avoid storing raw inputs by adding a loss term — *data-focused* (LwF, knowledge distillation: a teacher model supplies pseudo-labels for old tasks) or *prior-focused* (EWC, VCL: estimate per-weight importance and penalize changes to important weights). (3) **Architectural**: grow/freeze sub-networks per task. Because labels are scarce, this combines with **semi-supervised** learning (self-training, co-training, active learning, label propagation).

**Why it matters for AmI.** Users and environments evolve (health, habits, sensing setups), labels are scarce, and not all activities can be foreseen — so models must adapt continually with constant memory, open class sets, few-shot ability, and graceful forgetting.

**Exam angle.** Name the change type behind a scenario; define catastrophic forgetting and the stability–plasticity dilemma; classify a method (replay vs regularization vs architectural) and place EWC (prior-focused) and LwF (data-focused/distillation) correctly; explain why re-training from scratch fails.

title: Transfer Learning
body:
**Idea.** Transfer learning extends what is learned in one context to a new one, transferring knowledge from a (large, labeled) **source** to a (small) **target** to spend less time learning new tasks, need fewer human annotations, and handle more situations. It only makes sense when source and target are connected. A **domain** is the feature space + marginal distribution of the data; a **task** is the label space + predictive function — transfer addresses *differences* between source and target domains/tasks (whereas if they were identical, transfer would be unnecessary).

**Taxonomy.** **Data-based** transfer adjusts/transforms the *data*: **instance-based** reweights source samples (higher weight to those most similar to target) and reuses them to train the target; **feature-based** finds a low-dimensional latent representation reducing the domain gap, either *symmetric* (a common latent space for both) or *asymmetric* (transform only source samples toward the target). **Model-based** transfer exploits the *model*: **model control** adds regularizers so source knowledge flows into the target during training (knowledge distillation, domain adaptation); **parameter control** reuses source parameters to bootstrap the target — the most common case being **fine-tuning** (initialize with source weights, keep training on target data, vs. pure feature extraction where the backbone is frozen).

**Domain adaptation.** **Unsupervised domain adaptation** transfers from a labeled source to an *unlabeled* target by learning domain-invariant, class-discriminative features: a discriminator `D` classifies the domain and is fought adversarially so it *cannot* tell source from target (Discrepancy Loss), while classification is learned on the source (Classification Loss). A **GAN** variant generates target-like data from labeled source data (proved effective for cross-subject HAR).

**Pitfalls.** Two factors are necessary: sufficient **source–target similarity** and sufficient **target data**; otherwise **negative transfer** hurts target performance.

**Use in HAR / AmI.** Many labeled datasets exist but in specific settings, so transfer reuses them across **subjects, environments, positions, devices, and domains** to minimize new labeling. **Self-supervised learning** is framed as transfer with an unlabeled source: a pretext (upstream) task creates labels from data, then the feature extractor is fine-tuned on a small labeled downstream task.

**Exam angle.** Place a technique in the data-/model-based taxonomy; distinguish instance- vs feature-based and model- vs parameter-control; explain the discriminator and the two losses in domain adaptation; state the two necessary factors and define negative transfer; identify the HAR transfer scenario (cross-subject/-position/-device/etc.).

title: Federated Learning
body:
Federated Learning (FL) is a collaborative way to build a shared model across many users without centralizing their data. Contrast three options. In the **centralized** approach every client sends its raw labeled data to a server that pools it into one training set; this hurts **privacy** (sensor data can reveal habits/health) and **scalability** (latency from large transfers, heavy central compute). In **distributed on-site learning** training and inference stay on each device, which protects privacy but suffers from few local samples, no benefit from peers, and poor generalization. **Federated Learning** combines their strengths: each selected client trains locally and shares only **model parameters** (never raw data); the server aggregates them into a global model.

The FL process runs in **communication rounds**: the server selects available clients, sends the global model, clients train locally and return parameters, and the server aggregates - repeating until convergence. **FedSGD** (naive) has each client compute one average gradient and the server applies one SGD step. **FedAvg** (standard) has each client run several local SGD steps and send weights, which the server averages (weighted, e.g. by sample count); it costs more local computation but converges in far fewer rounds - valuable since communication is the bottleneck.

**Client selection** is needed because there may be thousands of clients and not all are available; the baseline is **random sampling** of available devices, but **utility-based** selection uses *statistical utility* (samples, local loss, local-vs-global difference) and *system utility* (training/transmission time, to avoid stragglers). **Synchronous** FL waits for all selected clients; **asynchronous** FL (e.g. FedAsync) aggregates updates as they arrive and weights them by **staleness** (`t - tau`): older updates contribute less.

The key challenge is **non-IID** data: each client reflects one user, giving feature, label, and quantity skew. Solutions include single-global-model **personalization** (share general input layers, fine-tune personalized output layers; ProtoHAR adds class prototypes), and **Federated Clustering** (FedCLAR), which groups similar clients via cosine similarity over model weights and trains one model per cluster. FL also preserves privacy but only mitigates it - shared weights can still leak information. AmI applications: Human Activity Recognition, smart energy, pervasive healthcare, smart cities/pollution, autonomous driving, GBoard. **Exam angle:** be ready to contrast centralized/distributed/federated, explain FedAvg vs FedSGD, describe a round, and link non-IID skews to personalization vs clustering.

title: Explainable AI (XAI)
body:
Explainable AI (XAI) makes ML models reveal the reasoning behind their predictions. In Ambient Intelligence, model outputs drive important decisions (healthcare diagnosis, smart-city management, energy choices, helping data scientists), so it matters not only *what* is predicted but *why*: knowing the 'why' helps understand the problem and data and anticipate when a model fails. XAI is not only about models - **HCI** and psychological factors matter, because explanations must be effectively *delivered* to their **target users**: non-expert end-users (clinicians, operators, monitored subjects) versus technicians (who tune the sensing setup, data, or model). The same prediction may need very different explanations.

Two key axes: **global** (how the model predicts in general, which features/interactions matter) vs **local** (why one instance got its prediction - the most widely used, as it is more accurate); and **interpretable-by-design** (parameters themselves explain) vs **post-hoc** (interpret an already-trained model). There is also an **accuracy vs interpretability** trade-off.

The course covers three categories. (1) **Interpretable models**: linear regression (weights = global importance), decision trees (variance-reduction importance globally, root-to-leaf path locally), and rule induction (IF-THEN rules). Simple, but often too weak for complex tasks. (2) **Model-agnostic** methods treat the model as a black box, working with any model: **LIME** perturbs the input, queries the model, and fits a similarity-weighted interpretable surrogate (local fidelity); **counterfactuals** find the smallest realistic feature change that flips the prediction. (3) **Deep explanation / neural-network interpretation**: **GradCAM** back-propagates the class gradient to the last convolutional layer and weights feature maps into a heatmap; **prototype-based** networks (autoencoder + prototype layer) classify by latent-space L2 distance to learned prototypes, visualized via the decoder. Post-hoc methods are criticized because explanations come from a separate process with strong priors and can change with the explainer; deep-explanation builds interpretability into the network.

**Evaluating explanations** uses user satisfaction, mental model, trust assessment, and correctability - because convincing explanations can mask model mistakes, causing **over-reliance**. XAI matters in AmI/health since explanations support trust and error correction in high-stakes, often time-series sensor settings. **Exam angle:** classify any method along global/local, agnostic/specific, post-hoc/interpretable; explain LIME and GradCAM mechanisms; and discuss evaluation measures and over-reliance.

title: Generative AI
body:
**Discriminative vs generative.** Discriminative models learn to label inputs; generative models learn the data distribution so they can *synthesize* new samples. In Ambient Intelligence this matters mainly for **data augmentation** and producing **synthetic sensor/HAR data** when labeled data is scarce.

**The families.**
- **GANs** (2014): a *generator* synthesizes samples and a *discriminator* learns to separate real from synthetic; the generator learns to fool it. High-quality samples but **low diversity due to mode collapse**, and hard to train. **Conditional GANs (CGAN)** condition generation on a class, so one model serves all classes (e.g., ActivityGAN for sensor HAR).
- **VAEs.** A plain AutoEncoder is a poor generator because its latent space overfits and lacks regularity. A VAE's encoder outputs a *distribution* (mean, std); a **KL loss** pulls the latent toward $N(0,I)$, regularizing it. Result: easy to train, high diversity, but lower fidelity. Conditional VAEs add a conditioning modality (e.g., text).
- **Diffusion models.** Forward diffusion adds noise; the model learns **backward diffusion** to denoise step by step, generating from pure noise (often via a **U-Net** whose skip connections let the decoder reuse input info). High-fidelity, high-diversity, easy to train, but **slow to sample**. **Latent diffusion** runs in a compressed space and is conditioned via cross-attention in the U-Net plus a conditional VAE.
- **Autoregressive / Transformers / LLMs.** Seq2seq models pre-trained by next-token prediction; usable for text and time series.

**Summary trade-off:** GAN = fidelity but mode collapse; VAE = diversity but blurry; Diffusion = best quality but slow.

**LLMs in AmI.** Smart-home sensor events are turned into sentences ('the fridge has been opened'); ADL-LLM and similar methods prompt an LLM (with temporal, room-layout and activation info) to infer the activity. **TDOST** uses textual sensor descriptions to improve cross-home transfer (semantics over raw patterns); **NLS** learns a shared sensor-text space for zero-shot HAR. Raw continuous signals are hard for LLMs, so few-shot, fine-tuning, **reprogramming (Time-LLM)** and foundation models are proposed. **IMUGPT/T2M-GPT** generate labeled inertial data by going text to 3D motion (VQ-VAE + autoregressive) to synthetic IMU.

**Privacy angle.** Synthetic data can replace sensitive real recordings, but generation has limits (approximation, scalability).

**Exam angle.** Know each family's mechanism and trade-off, why plain AEs fail, the KL term, diffusion's forward/backward + U-Net, and how sensor data is turned into text for LLM-based HAR.

title: Graph Neural Networks
body:
**Why graphs for AmI.** A graph $G=(V,E)$ is a set of nodes and their edges. The formalism is powerful because it captures *relationships* between data points, and it fits Ambient Intelligence naturally: sensor/IoT networks have related devices, and a multivariate time series has dependencies both between variables and across time. Edges may be undirected (symmetric adjacency matrix), directed (possibly non-symmetric) or weighted; **multi-relational** graphs type the edges and **heterogeneous** graphs also type the nodes. Each node carries features (node attributes $X\in\Re^{|V|\times m}$).

**The permutation problem.** Feeding a flattened adjacency matrix to an MLP fails: node ordering is arbitrary, so the representation is not **permutation-invariant** — **isomorphic** graphs get totally different matrices. GNNs aim for a permutation-independent representation by treating neighbors as an unordered *set*.

**Message passing.** The core mechanism: each node's embedding $h^{(k)}_u$ is updated by **AGGREGATE** (combine neighbor representations — a set function, for permutation invariance) and **UPDATE** (fold in the node's own state); both are differentiable (e.g., MLPs). Starting from $h^{(0)}_u = x_u$, after $K$ iterations a node encodes its $K$-hop neighborhood — analogous to stacking CNN layers, but over graph neighbors. Plain summation is unstable across different neighbor counts, so **(symmetric) normalization** is used.
- **GCN** = self-loops (absorbing UPDATE) + symmetric normalization. Self-loops simplify and reduce overfitting, but limit expressivity (can't separate self from neighbors).
- **Set pooling / Janossy pooling** are more expressive aggregations; **GraphSAGE** and attention-based **GAT** are common variants (GAT weights neighbors via attention).
- **Graph pooling** aggregates node embeddings into a single graph embedding $z_G$ (optionally attention-weighted).

**Tasks.** *Node classification* (per-node label, transductive/semi-supervised vs inductive — ignore test nodes in training); *link prediction* (infer missing edges, framed as metric learning on embeddings); *graph classification/regression/clustering* (one label per graph via $z_G$).

**AmI applications.** CNNs (grid graphs) and RNNs (line graphs) are special cases; GNNs handle variable-size, unordered, variable-degree graphs. Examples: body-sensing graphs for wearable HAR, spatio-temporal graphs, smart-home HAR (edges between temporally consecutive events — GNN-XAR), load disaggregation (NILM), indoor localization (APs as nodes, RSSI features).

**Exam angle.** Explain permutation invariance, AGGREGATE/UPDATE, GCN = self-loops + symmetric normalization, the three task types, and one or two AmI graph constructions (nodes/features/edges).
